item.title: Life after Bangabhaban: What former presidents get
Former presidents who left office before completing their terms
item.description:
Bangladesh’s 22nd President Mohammed Shahabuddin resigned on Friday, citing serious health complications, becoming the latest head of state to leave office before completing his constitutional term. His resignation letter was submitted to Speaker Hafizuddin Ahmed on Friday afternoon.
Since independence, Bangladesh’s presidency has been shaped by assassinations, military coups, constitutional changes, mass uprisings, and political crises. Of the 18 individuals who have served as president in a full, acting, or in-charge capacity, 10 left office before completing their terms.
Shahabuddin joins a list of presidents who resigned, were removed from office, lost power through military coups, or left because of political disagreements, mass protests, or health complications. Two sitting presidents were assassinated, while only one died of natural causes in office.
Justice Abu Sayeed Chowdhury, Bangladesh’s 3rd president, assumed office on January 12, 1972, after Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman became prime minister under the parliamentary system.
He resigned on December 24, 1973, before completing his tenure, reportedly following disagreements with the government on several issues. Mohammad Mohammadullah subsequently assumed the presidency.
Mohammad Mohammadullah first assumed the presidency in an acting capacity on December 24, 1973, following the resignation of Justice Abu Sayeed Chowdhury. He was sworn in as the country's fourth president on January 27, 1974.
His tenure ended on January 25, 1975, when Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman returned to the presidency after Bangladesh adopted a presidential system under the Fourth Amendment and the formation of BAKSAL.
Khondaker Mostaq Ahmad assumed power following the assassination of Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman and most members of his family on August 15, 1975. He declared himself president and led the government for 81 days.
During the Liberation War, Mostaq served as minister for foreign affairs, law, and parliamentary affairs in the Mujibnagar government. After independence, he held several cabinet portfolios under Sheikh Mujibur Rahman and later became a member of the executive committee of BAKSAL.
His presidency ended on November 6, 1975, when he was removed from power in a military coup.
Chief Justice Abu Sadat Mohammad Sayem became Bangladesh’s president on November 6, 1975, after Khondaker Mostaq Ahmad stepped down. He formally assumed office on November 8 and became the country’s first civilian president under martial law.
Sayem simultaneously served as Chief Martial Law Administrator (CMLA) before relinquishing that role on November 29, 1976.
He resigned as president on April 21, 1977, citing deteriorating health.
Following the assassination of President Ziaur Rahman in May 1981, Vice President Abdus Sattar became acting president before winning the presidential election on November 15, 1981, securing around 66% of the vote. The election, however, remained controversial.
His presidency ended on March 24, 1982, when Army Chief Hussain Muhammad Ershad staged a military coup. At the time, Sattar declared that he was unable to continue performing presidential duties because of ill health, effectively relinquishing office.
General Hussain Muhammad Ershad assumed the presidency on December 11, 1983, after removing President Ahsanuddin Chowdhury from office.
During his rule, Bangladesh held general elections in 1986 and 1988, both of which drew criticism over their credibility.
As opposition to military rule intensified, major political parties united in a nationwide movement demanding Ershad’s resignation. He resigned on December 6, 1990, following the mass uprising, ending nearly 9 years of military-backed rule.
AQM Badruddoza Chowdhury assumed office as Bangladesh’s 15th president on November 14, 2001, but resigned on June 21, 2002, after around 7 months in office amid mounting pressure from the ruling Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP).
Party lawmakers reportedly threatened to impeach him over several issues, including his decision not to pay tribute at former president Ziaur Rahman’s grave on his death anniversary and not referring to Zia as the “proclaimer of independence” in an official message. His visit to Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman’s mausoleum was also reported to have contributed to the party’s dissatisfaction.
Bangladesh’s political history has also witnessed the deaths of sitting presidents while in office.
Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, the country’s first president, was assassinated along with most members of his family in a military coup on August 15, 1975, while serving as president.
President and Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) founder Ziaur Rahman was assassinated during a military coup in Chittagong on May 30, 1981.
President Zillur Rahman remains the only Bangladeshi president to have died of natural causes while in office. Bangladesh’s 19th president died on March 20, 2013, while undergoing treatment at Mount Elizabeth Hospital in Singapore.
Mohammed Shahabuddin’s resignation adds another chapter to the history of Bangladesh’s presidency, an office that has frequently been shaped by political upheaval, military intervention, institutional tensions, and health-related departures.

From Pudong to Dhaka: Tale of two cities
item.description:
Over a decade before South Asia gained its first underwater expressway—the Karnaphuli Tunnel in our port city of Chittagong—I experienced under-river travel for the very first time during a road trip from Puxi to Pudong in China.
In the early winter of 2012, I left Puxi, the historic urban core of Shanghai on the west bank of the Huangpu River. Crossing the tunnel beneath the water, I landed on the Lujiazui peninsula.
Lujiazui, the prominent financial and commercial district on the eastern bank, immediately greeted me with its strikingly high skyline. I kept looking up, neck bent and chin raised, as the skyscrapers of the Pudong New Area left me awestruck.
Up until then, I had never seen such a dense concentration of tall buildings in a single city. I quickly realized that Pudong was a massive, sprawling district, and Lujiazui was just its crowning jewel—a hyper-modern neighborhood housing the financial hub.
Even after all these years, I vividly remember the sheer scale of the Shanghai Stock Exchange. Dressed in dark sunglasses, a green button-down shirt, and a dark blazer, I stepped onto the trading floor, where millions of Yuan worth of shares must have already changed hands that day. The entrance to this bustling bourse featured large glass-panel facades, heavy structural pillars, revolving doors, and stepped granite stairs where visitors and professionals constantly passed through.
The writer standing in the Lujiazui financial district of Pudong, standing between landmark skyscrapers – 1,380 feet Jin Mao Tower and 1,614 feet Shanghai World Financial Center. Photo: Collected/Dhaka Tribune
Stepping back out onto the streets from one of the world’s busiest financial hubs, the architecture continued to command my attention. At one point, I stood directly between two of China's most iconic supertall skyscrapers.
On one side rose the 88-story Jin Mao Tower, featuring a traditional Chinese pagoda-inspired tiered design that tapers gently to 1,380 feet. On the other stood an even taller, sleek, dark-blue glass giant—the 1,614-foot Shanghai World Financial Center, famously nicknamed "The Bottle Opener" due to the large trapezoidal aperture at its peak designed to reduce wind pressure.
Reflecting on Shanghai's incredible urban development, I eventually stopped at the base of the iconic Oriental Pearl Tower. Famous for its futuristic design featuring 11 spheres of varying sizes, the 1,535-foot structure was the tallest in China from its completion in 1994 until 2007. Hungry by this point, I caught the fast-moving elevator to dine at the revolving restaurant inside the tower's lower pink-and-silver sphere.
After a delicious lunch and a great dining experience, I walked out onto the famous glass-bottom skywalk of the observation deck. Standing on the outdoor transparent glass module 850 feet in the air, I looked directly down at the plaza and streets below.
From this vantage point, the distinctive pointed roof of the Bank of Shanghai Headquarters and the surrounding skyscrapers were sharply visible, while the Huangpu River snaked through the landscape far below. Before leaving, I also caught a fascinating sneak peek of the Shanghai History Museum situated directly underground.
Remarkably, about five years after witnessing these urban marvels, I had a rare chance to meet one of the masterminds behind Shanghai’s transformation. What made it even more special was that the meeting took place in my own hometown—Dhaka.
In 2017, the World Bank brought Professor Zhu Ruolin, a renowned urban planner and former dean of the Pudong Planning and Design Institute, to Bangladesh. The goal was to leverage his expertise for the future expansion and new town development of Dhaka, which has become one of the world’s most populous, congested, and underserved cities.
Professor Zhu Ruolin. Photo: Collected/Dhaka Tribune
Professor Zhu had been an instrumental figure in the master planning, architectural zoning, and urban development of Pudong, transforming it from a patchwork of paddy fields and farmland in the early 1990s into a global financial hub. Under his leadership at the institute, Shanghai expanded eastward across the Huangpu River, eventually emerging as the world's most populous city.
The writer along with a local host standing in front of the primary entrance plaza of the Oriental Pearl Tower in Shanghai, China. Behind them the distinctive large red greeting wall features the gold lettering “欢迎您” (Huānyíng nín) – Welcome. Located in the Pudong New Area of Shanghai, Oriental Pearl TV Tower stands right at the tip of the Lujiazui peninsula, the prominent financial district on the eastern bank of the Huangpu River. Photo: Collected/Dhaka Tribune
It was deeply rewarding to speak with this visionary man, who expressed confidence that Dhaka, too, could be transformed into a highly developed city. He explained that Pudong—China's answer to Manhattan—now boasts one of the world's leading stock exchanges, a major international airport, and a futuristic skyline because of an intentional multi-billion-dollar facelift that began during China's economic opening in the 1980s.
Visitors posing together outside the Jin Mao Tower plaza area in the Lujiazui financial district of Pudong, Shanghai. The tall totem sign in the center background reads J·LIFE, a high-end retail, dining, and lifestyle center located inside the podium of the Jin Mao Tower. Photo: Collected/Dhaka Tribune
In 1990, leaders in Beijing unveiled plans to develop the area east of the river into a Special Economic Zone, and three years later, the "Pudong New Area" was officially born.
Zhu then told me: “In 1992, Pudong was an underdeveloped area. In order to develop the area, we welcomed foreign investment and interest there.” He had said the authority invited five designers to submit their ideas to transform Pudong into a modern city. “We didn't accept or reject any submission. We prepared a fresh design taking all the good points from all five.”
Ever since my visit to Pudong in 2012 and my meeting with Professor Zhu in 2017, I have found myself wondering: how long will it take for us to see a successfully planned urban expansion in Purbachal? Why is it so difficult to transform Dhaka the way they transformed Shanghai?
Professor Zhu's words still echo in my ears today. He emphasized that everything they achieved in Pudong was driven by visionary thinking, well-thought-out plans, effective management, and coordinated efforts.
“A city is like a human body," he told me. "If you don't take care of it, she will get sick.”
Looking at the state of our capital today, I cannot help but ask: isn't Dhaka sick enough yet?
The main entrance to the Shanghai Stock Exchange Building, located in Pudong, Shanghai, China. Photo: Collected/Dhaka Tribune

Phone line, security in place as Shahabuddin prepares to leave
item.description:
A new telephone line is being installed at the Gulshan residence where outgoing President Mohammed Shahabuddin is expected to move after leaving Bangabhaban.
On Friday afternoon, employees of the Bangladesh Telecommunications Company Limited (BTCL) arrived to install the connection.
According to security guards at the building, Shahabuddin is expected to reside on the fifth floor after stepping down. They said he visited the residence 3 to 4 days ago, accompanied by more than 100 people, including members of law enforcement agencies.
Sources said minor renovation and other preparation work at the residence has already been completed. Several members of the Special Branch (SB) are also stationed there.
Abdur Razzak, a Telecommunications Mechanic (TCM) at Bangladesh Telecommunications Company Limited (BTCL), said the installation was being carried out following official instructions. He said: “We have officially been sent here to install a new telephone line. The previous line was disconnected after he became President. Therefore, we are now installing a new one.”
President Mohammed Shahabuddin resigned from office, and his resignation letter was accepted by Speaker of the National Parliament Hafiz Uddin Ahmed. The letter was submitted to the speaker on Friday.
In the resignation letter, Shahabuddin said he was stepping down due to serious illness.

item.title: A visit to born-again Kobe: Voices of the Kataribes and Kobe Shimbun’s resilience
Dengue cases surge sharply in Chittagong
item.description:
Dengue infections have risen sharply in Chittagong with the onset of the monsoon, as the number of cases recorded in the first 23 days of July has already exceeded the total reported during the previous six months.
According to the Chittagong Civil Surgeon’s Office, 356 people were hospitalized with dengue between July 1 and July 23, compared with 298 cases recorded from January through June. Overall, 654 dengue patients have been admitted to hospitals in the district between January 1 and July 23, while two people have died from the disease.
Health officials attributed the sharp increase to continuous rainfall, stagnant water, and the rapid breeding of Aedes mosquitoes, warning that infections could rise further unless mosquito control measures are strengthened and public awareness improves.
Data show that July has become the worst month of the year so far, with 356 infections recorded in just 23 days, compared with 122 in June, 37 in May, 29 in April, 20 in March, 22 in February, and 68 in January. Of the total patients, 260 are residents of Chittagong city, 262 are from the district’s upazilas, and 132 are from other districts under the division, while 376 are men, 165 are women, and 113 are children.
A recent survey by Chittagong Divisional Health Department found Aedes mosquitoes in every ward of the city and identified eight wards as the most vulnerable to dengue transmission: North Kattali (Ward 10), Pahartali (Ward 3), Alkaran (Ward 2), West Bakalia (Ward 17), South Bakalia (Ward 19), South Halishahar (Ward 39), Patharghata (Ward 34), and Andarkilla (Ward 32).
The survey showed that 70–80% of the mosquitoes collected were Aedes aegypti, the primary carrier of the dengue virus, while the remaining 20–30% were Aedes albopictus. Among the district’s 15 upazilas, Sitakunda reported the highest number of infections with 64 cases, followed by Lohagara and Raozan with 23 each, Patiya and Mirsharai with 18 each, Banshkhali with 16, Satkania with 15, Anwara with 13, Rangunia with 11, Hathazari with 8, Chandanaish with 7, Sandwip with 6, Fatikchhari with 4, and Boalkhali with 2 cases.
Chittagong Civil Surgeon Dr. Jahangir Alam said dengue is a mosquito-borne disease and stressed that eliminating mosquito breeding sites remains the most effective way to curb transmission. He said all government hospitals have been equipped with the necessary medicines and treatment facilities, while the Health Department has completed preparations to cope with a possible increase in patients.
City Corporation officials said mosquito control operations have been intensified across all 41 wards, with a trained 60-member team deployed to spray insecticides in hotspot areas and 205 spray workers conducting regular larvicide operations, while sufficient insecticide stocks have been secured for the next six months.

item.title: PM invites Qatari investment through letter to amir
item.description:
Prime Minister Tarique Rahman has invited Qatari investment in Bangladesh through a letter addressed to the amir of Qatar, as Dhaka seeks to further strengthen economic ties with the Gulf nation.
Foreign Minister Dr Khalilur Rahman handed over the letter to Qatar's Minister of State for Foreign Affairs Sultan bin Saad Al-Muraikhi during a bilateral meeting in Manila on Friday, said a foreign ministry's press release here.
The meeting was held on the sidelines of the 50th anniversary commemoration of the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia (TAC).
During the meeting, the two sides also discussed a wide range of bilateral issues, reflecting the two countries' interest in further advancing their longstanding relations.
The meeting took place as part of the Bangladesh foreign minister's diplomatic engagements in Manila surrounding the TAC anniversary commemoration.

item.title: Thailand likely to employ 10,000 Bangladeshis
item.description:
Bangladesh and Thailand have discussed the prospect of employment for around 10,000 Bangladeshi workers in Thailand following completion of necessary procedures, alongside expanding bilateral cooperation in trade, investment and tourism.
The issue was discussed during a bilateral meeting between Prime Minister's Foreign Affairs Adviser Humaiun Kobir and Thai Deputy Foreign Minister Vijavat Isarabhakdi in Bangkok on Friday morning, according to a press release received here on Friday.
Humaiun held the meeting during a stopover in the Thai capital following his official visit to Manila, the Philippines.
During the talks, the two sides reaffirmed their commitment to further strengthening the longstanding friendly relations between Bangladesh and Thailand and expanding cooperation in the areas of mutual interest.
They reviewed the overall state of bilateral relations and discussed ways to enhance cooperation in trade and investment, labour mobility, tourism and people-to-people contacts.
The two sides particularly exchanged views on the prospect of opening employment opportunities for around 10,000 Bangladeshi workers in Thailand once the necessary procedures are completed.
The Foreign Affairs adviser also sought Thailand's continued support for Bangladesh's inclusion as an Asean Sectoral Dialogue Partner.
In response, the Thai deputy foreign minister assured that Thailand would continue its constructive engagement on the matter.
Bangladesh has been seeking Asean Sectoral Dialogue Partner status as part of its broader efforts to deepen institutional engagement with the Southeast Asian regional bloc and strengthen economic and strategic ties with its member states.
During the meeting, Humaiun, on behalf of the prime minister of Bangladesh, also invited the Thai prime minister to visit Bangladesh at a mutually convenient time.

item.title: Info minister: All religious communities enjoy equal rights in Bangladesh
item.description:
Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon has said citizens of all religious faiths in Bangladesh enjoy equal rights, emphasizing that the country's constitution leaves no scope for discrimination or division based on religion.
"Sanatan believers enjoy the exact same rights as Muslims, Christians, and Buddhists in this country. Our constitution does not divide people on the basis of religion. Those who attempt to create division in the name of religion or politics are enemies of the nation, enemies of religion, and enemies of the Almighty," he said.
The minister made the remarks while addressing as the chief guest at a discussion organized on the occasion of Sri Sri Jagannathdev's Ulto Rath Yatra at the Dhakeshwari National Temple in the capital on Friday.
Swapon highlighted that the Sanatan faith represents a thousand-year-old heritage and history bears witness to its long-standing continuity.
"Throughout human civilization, spiritual leaders across all faiths are striving to reach the Almighty. True believers respect and revere followers of other faiths because everyone is ultimately seeking the same Supreme Power, regardless of the ritual or path they follow," he added.
The minister urged everyone to stay vigilant against forces attempting to exploit religious sentiments for narrow political gains or societal division, both domestically and regionally.
"Staying firm in one's own faith while showing deep respect to other faiths to ensure peaceful coexistence is the true essence of a non-communal and inclusive society," Swapon stressed.
He further underscored that under the philosophy of Bangladeshi nationalism—as outlined by Prime Minister Tarique Rahman—no division based on religion, caste, language, or geography will be tolerated.
Inaugurating the event, Dhaka South City Corporation (DSCC) Administrator Abdus Salam stated that Bangladesh belongs equally to all communities.
"Those who seek to undermine communal harmony do not truly believe in Bangladesh's sovereignty. People of the Hindu faith fought alongside us during our Liberation War, and this nation was achieved through shared sacrifices. There is no scope for anyone to be considered a second-class citizen here; every citizen shares equal rights," Salam noted. Bangladesh Puja Udjapan Parishad President Basudeb Dhar also spoke on the occasion as special guest.

item.title: Khalilur: Bangladesh to work closely for stronger Asean
item.description:
Foreign Minister Dr Khalilur Rahman has reaffirmed Bangladesh’s commitment to working closely with Asean and its partners to strengthen regional cooperation in tackling emerging and transnational threats.
Addressing the commemorative ceremony marking the 50th anniversary of the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia (TAC) in Manila, he stressed the need for practical and coordinated initiatives to bolster global resilience against pandemics, climate shocks, cyber incidents and supply chain disruptions, said a foreign ministry press release here on Friday.
Dr Khalilur said joint early-warning systems, shared risk assessments and regional crisis-response mechanisms could play an important role in strengthening collective preparedness and resilience against such challenges.
The foreign minister also outlined ways to address emerging threats posed by AI-enabled cybercrime as well as misinformation and disinformation spread through social media.
The TAC, signed five decades ago, establishes principles governing relations among states in the region, including mutual respect for sovereignty and territorial integrity and non-interference in internal affairs.
Bangladesh acceded to the TAC in 2007, becoming one of the first South Asian countries to formalize a treaty-based commitment to Asean’s normative framework.
On the sidelines of the TAC anniversary events, Dr Khalilur Rahman also held separate bilateral meetings with the foreign ministers of Chile, the Netherlands and the Philippines, as well as Qatar’s state minister for Foreign Affairs.
The meetings covered a range of issues of mutual interest, including trade and investment, energy supply, water-resource management, legal migration and the circular economy.
Khalilur Rahman is currently visiting Manila to attend the 33rd Asean Regional Forum (ARF) Foreign Ministers’ Meeting and commemorative activities marking the 50th anniversary of the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia.

item.title: 181 more dengue patients admitted to hospitals
item.description:
Around 181 dengue patients were admitted to various hospitals in the last 24 hours across the country. During the same period, there was no report of death from dengue, according to the Health Emergency Operation Center and Control Room of the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS).
Among the patients hospitalized with dengue, 57 are in the Dhaka Division, 25 in the Mymensingh Division, 71 in the Chittagong Division, 16 in the Khulna Division, two in the Rajshahi Division, seven in the Barisal Division and three in the Sylhet Division.
As of July 24, this year, a total of 12,201 people have been hospitalized with dengue.
A total of 39 people have died from dengue so far this year.
A total of 11,081 dengue patients have been discharged from hospitals.
From January 1 to December 31, 2025, a total of 102,861 people were infected with dengue, and a total of 413 people died from the disease.

item.title: Dhaka among 10 districts at risk as monsoon intensifies
item.description:
Bangladesh is bracing for another spell of stormy weather as an active southwest monsoon intensifies over the country, prompting the Bangladesh Meteorological Department (BMD) to issue warnings of thunderstorms, gusty winds and heavy rainfall across large parts of the country.
On Friday, the BMD forecast temporary gusty or squally winds of 45-60km/h, accompanied by rain or thunderstorms, over Dhaka, Faridpur, Khulna, Barisal, Patuakhali, Noakhali, Comilla, Chittagong, Cox's Bazar and Sylhet. Inland river ports in these districts have been advised to hoist Local Cautionary Signal No. 1.
The latest warning comes amid an increasingly active southwest monsoon, which has already triggered intermittent rain in several regions over the past week and is expected to continue influencing weather conditions in the coming days.
For the capital, the BMD forecasts partly cloudy to cloudy skies with rain or thundershowers throughout the day. South to southeasterly winds are expected to blow at 10-15km/h, while daytime temperatures are likely to remain nearly unchanged.
Official observations showed Dhaka recorded a temperature of 25.2°C at 6am on Friday, with 98% relative humidity, reflecting highly moisture-laden conditions. The city received 8mm of rainfall during the previous 24 hours, while Thursday's maximum temperature reached 31.2°C.
Meteorologists said the combination of high humidity and recurring rainfall is likely to keep conditions uncomfortable despite slightly lower temperatures.
According to the BMD's 24-hour nationwide forecast, most places in Dhaka, Khulna, Barisal and Chittagong divisions, along with many areas of Rajshahi, Rangpur, Mymensingh and Sylhet divisions, are likely to experience light to moderate rain or thundershowers accompanied by temporary gusty winds.
The department also warned of heavy to very heavy rainfall in Khulna, Barisal and Chittagong divisions, raising the risk of urban flooding, waterlogging and localized flash floods.
In the Chittagong Hill Tracts and adjoining hilly areas, prolonged rainfall may further increase the risk of landslides.
Meteorologists attribute the current weather pattern to an active southwest monsoon drawing abundant moisture from the Bay of Bengal. Combined with local atmospheric instability, the system is creating favourable conditions for widespread cloud formation, thunderstorms and intense rainfall.
Bangladesh typically experiences short-duration but high-intensity downpours during the monsoon season, particularly in coastal and southeastern districts.
Meteorologist Dr Md Bazlur Rashid said rainfall activity is expected to remain above normal over the next several days.
"The monsoon is currently active over Bangladesh, and this is likely to sustain widespread rain and thunderstorms across many parts of the country," he said.
He added: "Heavy to very heavy rainfall remains possible, particularly in the southern and coastal districts. People using waterways and those staying in open areas should closely follow official weather advisories."
Experts warned that the impacts of the current weather system extend beyond heavy rainfall.
High-intensity downpours could overwhelm urban drainage systems, worsening waterlogging in Dhaka, Chattogram and other major cities. Low-lying areas may experience temporary flooding, while prolonged rainfall in hilly regions could heighten the risk of landslides.
Lightning also remains one of Bangladesh's deadliest monsoon hazards. Authorities have advised people to avoid open fields, rivers, tall trees and electrical poles during thunderstorms, while operators of small boats have been urged to suspend operations when cautionary signals are in force.
With the monsoon expected to remain active through the weekend, the BMD said it will continue issuing updated forecasts and warnings as conditions evolve, urging residents to remain vigilant and follow official weather advisories.

Oil passes $100 a barrel again: Why it's more serious this time
item.description:
Brent crude went back over $100 a barrel Thursday for the first time since May, as the conflict in the Middle East intensified.
This time, however, the impact on the world economy could be greater given the conflict shows no sign of ending and reserves have already been partially been drawn down.
The last time Brent crude -- the benchmark international oil contact -- broke above $100 a barrel was in March 12 during the first stage or the US-Israeli war on Iran.
The last time before that was when Russia launched its invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
This latest price surge has been fuelled in part by attacks on Saudi Arabian tankers in the Red Sea by pro-Iranian Houthi rebel forces in Yemen, which would expand the zone of conflict and could cut off the export of millions of barrels of oil.
In normal circumstances, the world economy consumes a little over 100 million barrels of oil daily.
In mid-July, the International Energy Agency (IEA) warned that world production was below pre-war levels by around 9.4 million barrels a day.
The threat to the Red Sea raises the stakes.
It is the alternative route that Saudi Arabia has been using since the Strait of Hormuz was closed to export its oil.
After the war began it stepped up shipments via pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, which has allowed it export some three quarters of its pre-war level.
"This workaround is now facing headwinds as the blockade and the threat of attacks on ships will make passage through the Red Sea less viable in the near-term," said Maya Senussi of economic advisory firm Oxford Economics.
Oxford Economics fears that if both the Red Sea and the Strait of Hormuz were effectively closed to traffic, the price of oil could eventually pass $160 a barrel.
"Much of the world's spare production capacity has already been used, while strategic and commercial oil inventories are lower than when the war began, leaving the market with fewer buffers against a prolonged supply disruption," said Janiv Shah, vice president of the Rysted Energy consultancy.
That leaves the markets with less room for manoeuvre, he pointed out.
On Tuesday, the IEA warned that the renewed fighting in the Middle East was increasing concerns over energy supplies.
But there were still cushioning factors, it added.
It cited increased exports from several countries and Saudi Arabia and United Arab Emirates oil reaching markets by alternative routes.
Producers including the Brazil, Kazakhstan, United States and Venezuela were increasing their production, and China was trying to cut its imports, it added.
And around 30 IEA countries had between more than a billion barrels of oil in reserve.
Earlier this month the Kpler consultancy calculated there was a record 1.35 billion barrels of "oil on water" -- product already at sea.
That helps limit further crude oil prices rises, it argued.
The head of French oil giant TotalEnergies was similarly phlegmatic when he announced the company's second quarter results Thursday.
"We have no problem with stocks to supply our refineries," he said.
European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde nevertheless expressed alarm Thursday at the Houthi attack on a Saudi vessel in the Red Sea.
The situation in the Red Sea "is clearly going to have an impact and is having an impact", she said. "We can see that on the price of Brent as it evolves almost by the hour."
And some of the bank's governors had already mooted a possible rates hike, she added.
Inflationary pressure could encourage central banks to maintain high rates or even raise them.
Energy costs, not just of oil but also of gas in winter, hit both production and consumption and have a knock-on effect on economic growth.

Dhaka air quality moderate Friday morning
item.description:
Dhaka, the capital of Bangladesh, recorded moderate air quality on Friday morning, ranking 41st among cities with the worst air pollution worldwide.
At 9:50am, the city's Air Quality Index (AQI) stood at 66, indicating moderate air quality, according to air quality monitoring platform IQAir.
A moderate AQI means the air quality is generally acceptable. However, people who are unusually sensitive to air pollution may experience minor to moderate health effects if exposed for prolonged periods.
Kinshasa in the Democratic Republic of the Congo topped the list of the world's most polluted cities with an AQI score of 166, followed by Kampala, Uganda, with a score of 164.
Both cities recorded air quality classified as "unhealthy."
The AQI is calculated based on the concentration of major air pollutants, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), sulphur dioxide (SO₂) and ozone (O₃).
According to the AQI scale, a score between 51 and 100 is considered moderate, while 101–150 is deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, 151–200 is unhealthy, 201–300 is very unhealthy, and 301 or above is classified as hazardous, posing serious health risks to the entire population.
Dhaka has long struggled with air pollution, with air quality typically deteriorating during the dry winter months and improving during the monsoon season due to rainfall and stronger winds.
The World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes around seven million deaths globally each year, mainly due to stroke, heart disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer and acute respiratory infections.

Mozaffar shown arrested in crimes against humanity case
item.description:
The International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) on Thursday ordered that Major (retd) Mozaffar Hossain, one of the prime accused in the former president Ziaur Rahman assassination case, to be shown arrested in a case over crimes against humanity committed in Feni during the July 2024 uprising.
The three-member ICT-2, chaired by Justice Nazrul Islam Chowdhury, also granted the prosecution two days to interrogate the accused.
It fixed September 23 for submission of the investigation report.
The other members of the tribunal are Judge Md Manjurul Bashid and Judge Nur Mohammad Shahriar Kabir.
Chief Prosecutor Md Aminul Islam represented the prosecution during the hearing.
Earlier in the day, Mozaffar was produced before the tribunal. He will remain in Dhaka Cantonment Jail.
The Detective Branch (DB) of Dhaka Metropolitan Police (DMP) arrested Mozaffar in the capital on July 15 after over 45 years on the run.
Later, he was handed over to the Military Police at Dhaka Cantonment as his trial in the Zia assassination case will be held under the military law.
Meanwhile, another accused in the crimes against humanity case, Lieutenant General (retd) Masud Uddin Chowdhury, was also produced before the tribunal.

item.title: Experts push Volunteer Peer Leader model for adolescent health
item.description:
A national consultation recommended institutionalizing the Volunteer Peer Leader (VPL) Model within Adolescent-Friendly Health Centers (AFHCs) to improve adolescents' access to inclusive, youth-friendly health services, particularly mental health support.
The consultation, organized by SERAC-Bangladesh, brought together policymakers, public health experts, development partners, youth representatives and government officials to discuss integrating the model into the country's public health system.
The meeting was chaired by Dr Syed Kamrul Islam, director (PHC & ITHC) at the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) and chair of the Technical Working Group.
In his welcome remarks, SM Shaikat, executive director of SERAC-Bangladesh, said: "The Volunteer Peer Leader Model offers an effective solution to the human resource shortages in health facilities."
He said trained Volunteer Peer Leaders provide basic counseling, essential adolescent health information and referral support, serving as a bridge between communities and health facilities through a government-approved training curriculum.
Md Nazmul Hasan, project manager of SERAC-Bangladesh, presented the rationale for the VPL Model, its key interventions and gaps in adolescent health services. He said the model improves adolescents' access to health information, basic counseling, referral services and adolescent-friendly healthcare.
During the discussion, participants said institutionalizing the model would require a clear implementation framework, well-defined terms of reference, policy endorsement and adequate financial planning.
They also noted that many adolescents remain reluctant to seek healthcare because of social and cultural barriers, adding that trained Volunteer Peer Leaders can provide counseling, health education, awareness and referrals while strengthening links between communities and health facilities.
Participants said initiatives are underway to establish Adolescent Focal Bodies at the upazila and district levels in line with the National Adolescent Health Strategy.
They added that integrating the VPL Model into this structure, supported by standardized training packages, implementation guidelines, policy briefs and financial analysis, would facilitate its adoption within the government health system. Gradual expansion of the model across all upazilas, they said, could improve adolescents' access to inclusive and adolescent-friendly health services.
Sharing field experiences, Volunteer Peer Leader Md Boni Amin from Barguna said VPLs receive foundation training on the National Adolescent Health Strategy, adolescent development, communication skills and group facilitation before conducting awareness sessions in schools and communities, encouraging adolescents to seek healthcare and facilitating referrals to health facilities.
The consultation was attended by senior officials from the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, DGHS, the Directorate General of Family Planning (DGFP), development partners, academics, members of the Technical Working Group, Volunteer Peer Leaders, representatives of government and nongovernment organizations, and members of the national media.

item.title: KU students lock admin building, boycott classes over hidden camera allegation
item.description:
Khulna University (KU) students on Thursday locked the administrative building and suspended all academic activities, demanding justice over allegations that a hidden mobile phone was used to secretly record videos inside a women's dormitory washroom.
The protest erupted after residents of Bijoy ’24 Hall discovered a mobile phone concealed inside a washroom on Wednesday night.
Students subsequently detained Imam Hasan, 24, son of the hall canteen owner, who had been working at the canteen for about two years. They accused him of secretly recording videos of female students.
According to students, the phone was spotted around 10:30pm, prompting suspicion and a search. They alleged that several secretly recorded videos were found on the device.
The protesters further claimed that Imam had been recording videos through a washroom ventilator for an extended period and sharing them in various Telegram groups. They also alleged that his wife was aware of the activities.
The incident sparked outrage among residential students, who raised concerns about their privacy and safety.
Students held a protest rally at Hadi Chattar at noon, demanding a fair investigation and exemplary punishment for those responsible.
They alleged that the university administration had failed to take effective action hours after the incident came to light and claimed that previous allegations of sexual harassment involving university teachers had also not been adequately addressed.
The protesters declared that all academic and administrative activities would remain suspended until a credible investigation is completed and those responsible are brought to justice.
Bijoy ’24 Hall Provost Prof Dr Selina Ahmed said the matter is being investigated.
“The investigation is ongoing. It would not be appropriate to comment before it is completed. Necessary information and actions will be shared after the investigation report is received,” she said.

item.title: Bandarban residents risk lives crossing flood-damaged bridge
item.description:
Thousands of residents in Kuhalong Union of Bandarban Sadar are enduring severe hardship as a bridge over the Camlong stream, damaged by repeated floods, remains unsafe and unusable for vehicles.
The bridge, which first collapsed during the devastating floods of 2019, has never been fully repaired. As a result, residents—including students from at least five educational institutions—have been forced to cross the damaged structure daily, risking their lives due to the lack of an alternative route.
According to local sources, Kuhalong Union is home to more than 13,000 people. Around 5,000 residents of Camlong Para, Bhangamura Para, Barua Para, Marma Para, Dhalu Para, Chemir Mukh and Goalia Khola rely on the bridge to travel to Bandarban town and transport fruits and vegetables to markets in Chittagong and other areas.
The recent floods, triggered by days of heavy rainfall across Bandarban, have further weakened the bridge. Large sections are broken, while layers of mud deposited by floodwaters have made even pedestrian movement hazardous.
“The bridge has become a death trap. People have been suffering for years, but we have no other way to travel,” said Thuy Nu Ching Marma, a resident of Camlong area.
Local jeep driver Md Alam said the bridge had remained damaged for years and was further battered by the latest floods.
“Vehicles can no longer cross the bridge safely, disrupting transport and trade in the area,” he said.
Another resident, Md Jahangir, expressed frustration over the prolonged delay in rebuilding the bridge despite its importance to thousands of people.
Kuhalong Union Parishad Chairman Mongpu Marma said the bridge's collapse has severely disrupted the movement of people and goods. He added that the matter has repeatedly been brought to the attention of the relevant authorities.
Responding to the concerns, LGED Bandarban Executive Engineer Pratipada Dewan said a contractor has already been appointed to construct a new bridge over the Camlong stream at a cost of Tk 5 crore.
“The project has been approved and construction work will begin soon. Once completed, the new bridge will significantly improve communication and transportation in the area,” he said.

item.title: Over 800 acres of cropland in Thakurgaon remain submerged for months
item.description:
More than 800 acres of agricultural land at Salandar Farm under Thakurgaon Sugar Mill have remained submerged for nearly four months, causing extensive crop losses and pushing hundreds of farmers toward financial hardship.
Farmers say prolonged waterlogging has damaged sugarcane, potato, rice and other seasonal crops on around 814 acres of leased farmland, threatening both local livelihoods and a key source of agricultural seed production.
According to farm authorities, 33 farmers cultivated sugarcane on 239 acres this season, while another 581 acres were used for various rabi and kharif crops. Most of the affected cultivators are landless or low-income farmers who lease land from the government-owned farm through annual tenders.
Farmers estimate that sugarcane cultivation costs around Tk115,000 per acre, with many relying on loans from sugar mills and non-governmental organizations to finance production.
Lutfar Rahman, a leaseholder at the farm, said his entire sugarcane field had been ruined after remaining under water for months.
“I borrowed money to cultivate sugarcane, but the crop has rotted due to prolonged waterlogging. Now I do not know how I will repay the loans,” he said.
Many other farmers reported similar losses, saying potato fields, rice seedlings and other crops had also been destroyed.
Farmers and local residents blame the situation on a neglected drainage canal that once carried excess water from the farm to the Dhepa River. They allege that years of silt accumulation, weed growth and lack of maintenance have blocked the canal, preventing water from draining even after moderate rainfall.
“Previously, rainwater from the farm flowed into the Dhepa River through the canal. Since that connection was lost, even a small amount of rainfall causes extensive waterlogging,” said Jalal Uddin, a farmer from the Sotapir area.
Despite repeated appeals, affected farmers say no effective steps have been taken to resolve the problem.
Agricultural experts and local stakeholders warn that the impact extends beyond individual farmers. Salandar Farm supplies sugarcane to Thakurgaon Sugar Mill and produces foundation and certified seeds of crops including potato, rice, chilli and pulses for distribution across the country.
Abdul Mannan, an agriculturist and leaseholder, described the situation as a “man-made disaster”.
“Regular maintenance and re-excavation of the drainage canal could have prevented this crisis. Farmers informed the authorities long ago, but no effective action has been taken,” he said.
Farmers estimate that nearly 1,000 acres of surrounding farmland outside the farm area are also affected by waterlogging. They believe re-excavating the canal and restoring its connection to the Dhepa River could protect around 2,000 acres of fertile land and significantly increase agricultural output.
The affected farmers have submitted a memorandum to the district administration, demanding urgent excavation of the canal and immediate measures to resolve the long-standing waterlogging problem. They warn that without swift intervention, both the local sugar industry and the country's seed production network could face serious setbacks.
Former Managing Director of Thakurgaon Sugar Mill Shahjahan Kabir said the authorities are concerned about the waterlogging situation at Salandar Farm.
“We are concerned about the persistent waterlogging at Salandar Farm. Initiatives are being taken to renovate the drainage canal as quickly as possible in coordination with the Bangladesh Water Development Board and the district administration,” he said.
Acting Managing Director Saiful Islam said he had only recently assumed office.
“I have just taken charge. After reviewing the matter, I will discuss it with all relevant stakeholders and take the necessary steps,” he said.

item.title: BNP leader Rafiqul Islam Miah on life support
item.description:
BNP Standing Committee member and former minister Rafiqul Islam Miah was placed on life support at Evercare Hospital Dhaka on Thursday evening after his condition deteriorated.
He was put on life support at 7:20pm, his Personal Secretary Muksedur Rahman Abir said.
He said the BNP leader’s kidneys are no longer functioning, and doctors detected blood infection. "He has been suffering from neurological problems for a long time and is now facing multiple complications," he added.
Rafiqul Islam was admitted to Evercare Hospital on July 21 and had been receiving treatment in a cabin equipped with ICU facilities. However, his condition began to worsen from around noon on Thursday, his personal secretary said.
He is currently under the care of specialist physician Dr Farid Hossain at the hospital.
Abir said the family has sought prayers from people across the country for Rafiqul Islam's recovery.

item.title: Bangladesh expects Delhi to address peacefully protests in India
item.description:
State Minister for Foreign Affairs Shama Obaed Islam on Thursday said Bangladesh expects India to address promptly and peacefully the concerns of its people, being the world’s largest democracy.
"As a democratic government and state, we would expect India, as the world's largest democracy, to take into account the position of its people and move quickly towards resolving the problem," she told a media briefing at the foreign office.
The junior minister made the comments while responding to a question on the current student-led protests in the neighbouring country, adding “Bangladesh wants peace and stability to prevail in the region”.
Asked for comments on media reports about President Mohammed Shahabuddin’s alleged telephone conversation with deposed prime minister Sheikh Hasina, she said the foreign ministry had not yet any specific information on the matter.
Shama, however, said the government would investigate the matter if any relevant information became available.

item.title: Call for greater awareness to protect women's mental and physical health
item.description:
Speakers at a seminar on Saturday stressed the need to ensure women's mental and physical well-being as a prerequisite for building a healthy society, calling for greater public awareness and easier access to modern healthcare services.
The seminar, titled "Protection of Women's Mental and Physical Health," was organised by the Bangladesh Nari Sangbadik Samity (BANASAS) at the National Press Club on Wednesday.
The chief guest, Prof Dr Sheikh Nazmul Huda, chairman of the Department of Public Health at the International University of Business Agriculture and Technology (IUBAT), highlighted the indications, benefits and potential risks of Caesarean section (C-section).
He also discussed the role of political decisions and public policies in shaping healthcare services.
He said hysterectomy is not always necessary for young women suffering from conditions such as fibroids, cysts or tumours, noting that advances in modern medical treatment often make it possible to preserve the uterus while providing effective care.
Former Associate Professor of Shaheed Suhrawardy Medical College and Hospital, Dr Yasmin Jahan Shaheen, called for a nationwide drive to expand cervical cancer screening, increase uptake of the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine and strengthen public awareness, warning that stigma and late diagnosis continue to claim preventable lives.
Dr Shaheen noted that cervical cancer usually develops gradually over a decade and often remains symptom-free in its early stages. She said the disease is largely preventable through HPV vaccination, ideally administered between the ages of 9 and 14, before exposure to the virus.
She added that treatment varies depending on the patient's condition and the stage of the disease, while early detection is possible through the simple Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid (VIA) screening test, which helps identify precancerous changes and enables timely treatment.
Zakia Shishir, convener of the Agrajatra Nari Forum and a human rights activist, said families and society have a critical role to play in safeguarding women's mental health, as women continue to face domestic violence, workplace harassment, social bullying and cyber abuse on a regular basis.
‘Such violence and abuse often leave women struggling with depression and psychological distress, adversely affecting their work, thinking and future aspirations. It also undermines their self-confidence, hindering both their personal and professional development.
Pharmacist and researcher Samin Fardeen Soumik delivered a presentation on osteoporosis, describing it as a "silent" bone disease that gradually weakens bones, leaving them fragile and prone to fractures from even minor falls or routine daily activities.
He said women are at greater risk of developing osteoporosis after menopause due to hormonal changes. Smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, physical inactivity and a family history of the disease are among the major risk factors.
“Osteoporosis can largely be prevented through a healthy diet, regular exercise, maintaining a healthy weight, avoiding smoking, ensuring adequate intake of calcium and vitamin D, and limiting excessive salt consumption,” he added.
Among others, Zauhar Iqbal Khan, general secretary of the Dhaka Sub-Editors Council; Shahjahan Swapan, welfare secretary of the Dhaka Union of Journalists; and Nahid Hasan, sports and cultural secretary of the Dhaka Sub-Editors Council, also addressed the seminar.
The programme was chaired by Nasima Akter Soma, President of the Bangladesh Nari Sangbadik Samity (BANASAS).

item.title: Rizvi questions who sheltered Zia assassination convict for 45 years
Info minister: Authority needed to identify journalists
item.description:
Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon on Thursday said there is a need for an appropriate authority to determine who qualifies as a journalist and who does not.
The minister made the remarks while delivering a directive speech at the 34th meeting of the Press Accreditation Committee and the inaugural meeting of the newly formed committee, held at the conference room of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting at the Bangladesh Secretariat in the capital.
He advised members of the Press Accreditation Committee to ensure that every decision is consistent with the Constitution, existing laws, and relevant rules and regulations.
He urged them to exercise foresight, wisdom, and adopt an inclusive approach that accommodates the largest possible number of people while making decisions.
He also reminded the committee that its decisions should be broadly acceptable.
The minister said committee members must work not as representatives of any political party but as responsible representatives of a government elected by the people, bearing equal responsibility toward all citizens.
He said the media is a pluralistic sector where there is no alternative to the coexistence of people with diverse opinions, perspectives, and ideas. Therefore, instead of making narrow or biased decisions, the committee should adopt an inclusive approach that accommodates the largest possible number of people.
"The true capability of political leadership lies in making acceptable and inclusive decisions, even in difficult and sensitive situations," the minister said.
Zahir Uddin Swapon said the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting is one of the state's most politically sensitive ministries. Even a minor wrong decision can have major political and social consequences in the future. Therefore, decisions should be made with due consideration of their long-term impact rather than focusing solely on immediate outcomes, he added.
He said the acceptability of the committee's decisions would depend on the wisdom, experience, and foresight of its members.
Principal Information Officer Syed Abdal Ahmed chaired the meeting, which was attended by Secretary (Routine Duty) of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting Md Shah Alam, senior ministry officials, and members of the Press Accreditation Committee.

item.title: Muhit: Bangladesh targets eliminating cataract blindness in 5 years
item.description:
State Minister for Health Dr M A Muhit on Thursday expressed optimism that Bangladesh would eliminate cataract blindness within the next four to five years through a coordinated national initiative involving the government, private sector and non-governmental organizations (NGOs).
"A nationwide cataract surgery program will be implemented for elderly citizens, with several hundred thousand successful operations to be carried out annually to eliminate cataract-related blindness within the next four to five years," he said.
He made the remarks while speaking as the chief guest at a stakeholder consultation workshop titled "Advanced Eye Care in Bangladesh: Action Plan for Reducing Visual Impairment and Preventing Blindness" at the National Institute of Ophthalmology and Hospital in Sher-e-Bangla Nagar in the capital.
An international public health and eye care specialist, Muhit said advanced eye care services powered by artificial intelligence (AI) and modern technology would be expanded to ensure quality treatment reaches poor and marginalized people as a basic citizens' right.
He said Bangladesh has made significant progress in eye care through the combined efforts of the government, private sector and NGOs, but the country still lacks a unified reporting system. To address the issue, he said the government is preparing a five-year national perspective plan incorporating a common reporting mechanism to strengthen eye care services across the country.
Under the plan, the first year will focus on reassessing existing eye care facilities and planning services at district hospitals, followed by measures to improve infrastructure, recruit additional manpower where necessary and enhance service capacity.
The state minister said the government also plans to introduce comprehensive eye care services for people of all age groups.
As part of the initiative, school-based screening programs will be launched to identify vision problems among children and provide corrective glasses, while workers in factories, offices and other institutions will receive regular eye health services and access to spectacles.
Highlighting the growing burden of diabetic retinopathy, Muhit said modern technologies have significantly improved the delivery of eye care services at the grassroots level.
He stressed that advanced eye care services should not remain confined to urban areas but must be made accessible to people living in remote and underserved regions.
Describing the shortage of skilled human resources as a major challenge, the state minister called for greater investment in training and capacity building for ophthalmologists and other eye care professionals.
He also underscored the need to expand both the number and quality of government and private training institutions.
Acting Director of the National Institute of Ophthalmology and Hospital Prof Dr A S M M Quadir also addressed the workshop, while representatives of the ARK Foundation and the World Health Organization (WHO) presented the keynote paper.

item.title: Man awarded death penalty for killing 3rd grader after rape in Comilla
item.description:
A tribunal in Comilla on Thursday sentenced a man to death in a case filed over the rape and murder of a nine-year-old child in Comilla in 2024.
The convict was identified as Mofizul Islam Mofu, son of the late Abdul Khalek of Khilpara village in Comilla Sadar Dakshin Upazila.
Judge Md Yasin Arafat of the Women and Children Repression Prevention Tribunal-1 delivered the verdict after completing the trial in just 19 working days.
According to the prosecution, on April 29, 2024, Tanjim Sultana Jhumur, a third-grade student at Sonali Shishu Bidyaniketan in Kheyaish village under Galiara Union of Comilla Sadar Dakshin Upazila, went missing while returning home from school.
When she failed to return home at the expected time, her family searched for her in every possible place. On information from local residents, they later recovered Jhumur's body from a paddy field in the Thakurbari area of the village.
Police also recovered her school bag, school uniform, and shoes from the spot.
The following day, the victim's mother, Razia Akhter, filed a case with Sadar Dakshin Police Station, naming Mofizul Islam Mofu, a resident of Khilpara village in Sadar Dakshin Upazila, as the sole accused.
Three days after the incident, members of the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB) conducted a raid in Chandpur and arrested the accused, Mofizul Islam Mofu.
Later, Mofizul confessed to raping and killing the child before the court.

item.title: State minister urges BIWTA to boost revenue, improve services
item.description:
State Minister for Shipping Md Razib Ahsan on Thursday directed the Bangladesh Inland Water Transport Authority (BIWTA) to further intensify its activities to improve service quality, expand revenue collection and make the country's inland water transport system more modern, safe and efficient. The directive came at a view-exchange meeting on "Enhancing the Authority's Revenue Generation and Service Improvement," held at the sixth-floor conference room of BIWTA's head office, with Ministry of Shipping Secretary Zakaria also present.
Mohidul Islam, Additional Secretary to the government and Chairman of BIWTA, opened the meeting with a PowerPoint presentation on the authority's revenue and income growth, service improvement measures and ongoing development activities, touching on dredging operations, maintenance of river navigability, river port management, leasing activities, revenue collection, ensuring water safety and digital transformation of services.
Following the presentation, the state minister issued a series of directions aimed at making BIWTA's operations more result-oriented and people-friendly.
He asked the authority to carry out dredging in a cost-effective manner, prioritising routes most in need of excavation, and to take up projects to make buoys, lights, beacons and navigational markers more visible across waterways to enhance safety of movement.
He also instructed BIWTA's Port Division to give special priority to quickly settling ghats that remain unresolved in leasing up to the seventh phase of the 2026-27 fiscal year, and called for updating foreshore and land-use fees to boost revenue alongside effective steps to expedite civil suits related to ghats and points.
The state minister further directed the authority to introduce automation in cargo loading and unloading at river ports in phases, take coordinated measures with all relevant stakeholders to ensure the safety of vessels plying the waterways, and identify potential new revenue sectors for the Port Division with quickly implementable initiatives.
Speakers at the meeting said BIWTA needs to be transformed into a more efficient, modern, technology-driven and service-oriented institution in line with the government's Smart Bangladesh vision, stressing the need to create new avenues for revenue generation while ensuring optimal use of existing resources.
The state minister and the secretary, at the end of the meeting, expressed hope that swift implementation of the directions would help BIWTA enhance service quality, expand revenue collection, ensure navigational safety and make the country's inland water transport system more effective and people-friendly.

item.title: ULAB scool of business concludes BBA Monsoon Expo 2026
item.description:
The ULAB School of Business (USB) successfully wrapped up its signature three-day BBA Monsoon Expo 2026 on Wednesday with a vibrant closing ceremony held at the main campus. Organized under the university's flagship Curriculum Integration Program for the Summer 2026 term, the expo celebrated the intersection of student-led academic innovation and seasonal radiance. The initiative brought real-world industry applications directly into the classroom environment across multiple foundational business courses.
The competitive expo featured extensive student engagement, drawing in around 300 students divided into roughly 35 innovative teams. An elite panel of judges evaluated the participants throughout the exhibition. The panel ultimately recognized 13 exceptional teams for their outstanding final projects and professional presentations. Winners were chosen based on rigorous criteria, including core project depth, exceptional teamwork skills, and inventive problem-solving.
The program cross-integrated multiple business pathways, highlighting critical core courses and specialized supply chain tracks. Dedicated faculty members guided the participating cohorts. The event also held a Corporate Monsoon Flashmob led by ULAB students with a Tagore song on its beautiful open campus.
The closing ceremony concluded with an inspiring corporate address titled "The Economy of Monsoon" delivered by Professor Milan Kumar Bhattacharjee, PhD, special adviser to the Board of Trustees and Treasurer at ULAB. Professor Sarwar Uddin Ahmed, dean School of Business delivered a speech and the event ended with a celebratory certificate distribution, an academic photoshoot, and a vote of thanks from Dr Wazir Ahmad, director of the BBA Program.

item.title: Infant killing: Police seek remand for father, hearing Sunday
item.description:
Police have sought a three-day remand for Kabir Islam, the father accused of killing his seven-month-old daughter by throwing her to the ground during a domestic dispute with his wife in Dhaka's Uttara area.
The remand petition was submitted on Thursday by the case's investigating officer and Sub-Inspector Mehedi Hasan Faisal of Turag Police Station.
Dhaka Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrate Zakir Hossain fixed Sunday for the hearing on the remand petition in the presence of the accused.
SI Helal Uddin of the prosecution division confirmed the matter.
According to the case documents, Kabir allegedly slammed his seven-month-old daughter, Rafa Moni Kobita, onto the ground on the evening of July 15 following an argument with his wife. Locals caught him and handed him over to police.
The following day, police produced him before the court after showing him arrested under Section 54 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, and the court ordered that he be sent to jail.
The infant succumbed to her injuries while undergoing treatment on July 16.
On July 17, the child's maternal grandmother, Sarmin Akter, filed a murder case with Turag Police Station against Kabir. Police later sought to show him arrested in the murder case, and the court granted the application on Tuesday before sending him back to jail.
According to the case statement, Kabir and Lima Akter were married two years ago, and Rafa Moni was their only child. Kabir, a rickshaw puller by profession, had allegedly been addicted to drugs for a long time.
The complaint alleges that Kabir frequently assaulted and verbally abused his wife after taking drugs. On July 15, Lima had gone to her father's home in Balur Math Harun Molla Basti with the baby to visit her ailing father.
Around 7:00 pm, an argument broke out between the couple over a family matter. Enraged, Kabir allegedly snatched the infant from her grandmother's arms, lifted her above his head, and hurled her onto the road.
The critically injured child was rushed to Dhaka Medical College Hospital, where she later died while receiving treatment.

item.title: ICT shows Mozzafar arrested in crimes against humanity case
item.description:
The International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) on Thursday showed Major (retd) Mozzafar Hossain, an accused in the assassination case of former President Ziaur Rahman, arrested in crimes against humanity case over atrocities allegedly committed in Feni during the July mass uprising. The tribunal also granted investigators two days to interrogate him.
A three-member bench of ICT-2, headed by Justice Nazrul Islam Chowdhury, passed the order following a prosecution petition.
Chief Prosecutor Md Aminul Islam appeared for the prosecution and sought permission to show Mozzafar arrested in the case, allow a two-day interrogation, and extend the deadline for submitting the investigation report.
After the hearing, the tribunal granted the prosecution's petition, formally showing Mozzafar arrested in the case and authorising investigators to interrogate him for two days at a time convenient to the investigation agency. The tribunal also fixed September 23 for the submission of the investigation report.
Mozzafar was produced before the tribunal earlier in the day and will remain in custody at the Dhaka Cantonment prison.
Lieutenant General (Retd.) Masud Uddin Chowdhury, another accused in the same case, was also produced before the tribunal.
According to the prosecution, evidence collected during the investigation suggests Mozzafar's involvement in various crimes against humanity during the Awami League government's tenure. Prosecutors alleged that he maintained regular communication with Masud Uddin Chowdhury during the killings in Feni amid the July mass uprising, leading to the application to show him arrested in the case.
On July 21, the prosecution sought a production warrant for Mozzafar, one of the accused in the Ziaur Rahman assassination case. After evading arrest for nearly 45 years, he was apprehended from a residence in Dhaka's Banani area on the night of July 15 and handed over to the Military Police at Dhaka Cantonment the following day.

item.title: Budget's success depends on implementation, says Khasru
item.description:
Finance Minister Amir Khasru Mahmud Chowdhury on Thursday said the true success of the budget depends on its effective implementation, urging officials of the Finance Division to work collectively to translate the fiscal blueprint into tangible outcomes.
Speaking at his first meeting with Finance Division officials at the Ministry of Finance, the minister said the budget, prepared through nearly two months of work, has received appreciation from people across the country.
“A well-prepared budget alone is not enough. Its real success depends on effective implementation,” he said.
Khasru expressed confidence that the Finance Division will be able to implement the budget through coordinated efforts.
Finance Division Secretary Dr Khairuzzaman Mozumder chaired the meeting.
The minister also called for changing the negative public perception of bureaucracy, saying he has realised over the past four months that any challenge could be overcome through teamwork.
He urged officials to move away from conventional approaches and embrace innovative thinking to keep pace with a rapidly changing world.
Khasru said official meetings should not remain one way, stressing the need to encourage open and spontaneous participation of officers.
He called for a work culture based on free discussion, mutual cooperation and “out-of-the-box” thinking, adding that the workplace should function like a family where officials focus on solutions instead of merely identifying problems.
The minister said objective and timely feedback from different wings of the Finance Division will facilitate better policy-making and decision-making.
Describing the Finance Division as the centre of the country’s economic management, he urged all officials to discharge their responsibilities with professionalism and dedication in the national interest.
Later, speaking to reporters after a meeting at the Secretariat on the integrated development plan for Cox’s Bazar, the minister said the government has initiated steps to prepare a comprehensive master plan to develop the coastal district into an international and regional tourism hub.
He said the government is also reviewing the long-standing 50 metre and 500 metre development restrictions in coastal areas to facilitate planned tourism infrastructure while ensuring environmental protection.
“Where tourism is developed, hotels, motels, resorts and recreational facilities will have to be established. Development opportunities must be created without compromising environmental conservation,” Khosru said.
He said the government has a broader vision for Cox’s Bazar, highlighting ongoing infrastructure projects, including the construction of an international airport, railway connectivity and improved road links from Chattogram.
The minister said these projects will be integrated to transform Cox’s Bazar into a modern digital tourism city.
He also said Bangladesh’s target of becoming a $1 trillion economy by 2034 will be supported by Cox’s Bazar as one of the country’s major growth hubs through the expansion of tourism, trade and service sectors.

item.title: 3 DIGs among 17 police officers sent into forced retirement
item.description:
The government has sent 17 police officers, including three deputy inspectors general (DIGs), into compulsory retirement.
The Ministry of Home Affairs issued a notification in this regard on Thursday.
Those who have been sent into retirement also include 12 additional DIGs and two officials holding the rank of superintendent of police.
According to the notification, the action was taken under Section 45 of the Public Service Act, 2018 in "public interest."

item.title: Measles outbreak: Death toll reaches 805 as two die in 24 hours
item.description:
Two more children have died from measles-like symptoms in the 24 hours leading up to 8:00am on Thursday, raising the overall death toll from the ongoing outbreak to 805.
The Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) has classified both of the latest casualties as suspected.
With the latest figures, the total number of suspected measles deaths has reached 710, while the number of confirmed deaths remained unchanged at 95, as no new confirmed measles deaths were reported in the last 24 hours.
Of the two new suspected fatalities, one was reported in the Sylhet division and the other in the Rangpur division. At the district level, the deaths occurred in Sunamganj and Gaibandha, with one casualty reported in each.
A total of 848 suspected measles cases were recorded in the 24 hours till Thursday morning, taking the overall suspected case tally to 121,200.
During the same period, 166 new confirmed measles cases were reported, bringing the total number of confirmed infections to 15,007.
Since March 15, 2026, a total of 103,863 suspected measles patients have been hospitalized across the country, of whom 100,188 have recovered and been discharged from hospitals, according to the DGHS.

item.title: New Navy Chief adorned with Vice-Admiral rank badge
Iran vows to continue strikes in Mideast war
item.description:
Iran vowed on Thursday to keep striking the region so long as it remains under attack, as the Middle East war spread to new fronts with Tehran’s Houthi allies attacking Saudi shipping in the Red Sea.
The war resumed following a ceasefire that lasted just a few weeks, throwing the region and global energy markets back into chaos as Iran and the United States battle for control of the vital Strait of Hormuz.
The Houthis this week joined the conflict, claiming to have struck two Saudi oil tankers after declaring a blockade of Saudi ports.
Meanwhile, Jordan and Kuwait both reported intercepting strikes as Iran’s military and Revolutionary Guards claimed to be targeting US assets in both countries.
“The armed forces’ retaliatory attacks will continue as long as the US attacks on the country’s infrastructure and coastal areas continue,” Iran’s army spokesperson Mohammad Akraminia said, according to state TV.
The developments followed fresh US strikes on Iranian military targets, with US Central Command reporting its twelfth consecutive night of strikes, saying it aimed to “further degrade Iran’s ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters.”
President Donald Trump on Wednesday said the US would bomb Iranian civilian infrastructure every time Tehran fires at ships in Hormuz.
“Any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT,” Trump wrote on social media.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran would respond in kind to any attack on its infrastructure, writing: “Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye.”
Iranian state media said Thursday that a US attack killed two people at Shalamcheh on the Islamic republic’s border with Iraq.
It also reported two US missiles hit Bushehr, home to the country’s only civilian nuclear plant and a frequent target of Washington’s strikes.
The Houthis said they had carried out missile and drone strikes on two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea, the Encelia and the Layla, after declaring a blockade of Saudi ports.
British naval security monitor Ukmto said the master of a tanker reported being hit by an unknown projectile about 70 nautical miles southwest of Al Shuqaiq, causing a fire that the crew was fighting.
Saudi Arabia confirmed the Encelia had been struck, without commenting on the other tanker.
It remains unclear how fully the Houthis could enforce a blockade, but the threat could compound the shock of the Hormuz closure, endangering Riyadh’s ability to bypass the strait for some oil exports.
In Hormuz, the Revolutionary Guards said they stopped three oil tankers from transiting, as the US and Iran vie for control over the route through which around one fifth of the world’s oil passed before the war.
“The Strait of Hormuz is under our control... and any ship that is deceived by the US and intends to pass without coordination with the Islamic Republic of Iran will suffer the same fate,” the Revolutionary Guards said.
Oil prices again climbed Thursday, with Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, up four percent at $97.80 a barrel and bringing back fears of spiraling consumer prices and slowed growth globally.
On the sidelines of a Southeast Asian summit in Manila, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the Houthis had been “suckered” into attacking Red Sea shipping by Iran.
“The Houthis largely were smart and stayed out of all this throughout the conflict, but they now apparently have gotten themselves suckered into this, going after Saudi Arabia and their ships,” Rubio said.
“I hope they will de-escalate, because I think the Houthis, frankly, got snookered into this thing by the Iranians.”
Oman, a key mediator in the longstanding war between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia, said it was working to resume talks between the two sides, as it voiced “great concern” at the situation in the Red Sea.
